question: The Operator Product Expansion (OPE) systematises short-distance behaviour of products of operators. State the OPE.

options: $A(x)B(0) \xrightarrow{x\to 0} \sum_n C_n(x) \mathcal O_n(0)$ â€” products factorise into Wilson coefficients $C_n(x)$ (short-distance, perturbatively computable) times local operators $\mathcal O_n$ (long-distance, non-perturbative matrix elements); ordered by canonical dimension
$A(x)B(0) = A(0)B(0) + O(x^2)$ â€” Taylor expansion in $x$ around $0$ (this describes a different phenomenon) (not supported by the evidence) (this conflates cause and effect)
$A(x)B(0) = \int d^4y\,K(x,y)A(y)B(y)$ â€” convolution with a kernel (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a common misconception) (this reverses the actual relationship)
OPE only applies to free field theory and breaks down in interacting QCD (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
passive.explanation: Wilson's OPE (1969): the product of two local operators at short separation can be expanded into a series of local operators times c-number coefficients. Dimensional analysis: $C_n(x) \sim x^{d_n - d_A - d_B}$ where $d_n$ is the canonical dimension of $\mathcal O_n$. Leading terms have lowest-dimension operators. Factorisation: $C_n$ depend only on short distances $\ll \Lambda_{\rm QCD}^{-1}$, calculable in perturbation theory; matrix elements $\langle\mathcal O_n\rangle$ encode hadronic physics, fit to data or computed on lattice. Essential for deep inelastic scattering, $\tau$ decays, $B$ physics.

active.prompt: Match each operator in a typical QCD OPE with its physical role:

pairs.definition: Leading term; coefficient = perturbative result; OPE corrections refine this
Lowest non-trivial condensate; signals spontaneous chiral breaking; $\sim -(250 \text{ MeV})^3$
Non-perturbative vacuum gluon density; $\sim 0.012 \text{ GeV}^4$ from SVZ sum rules
Quark-gluon condensate $\sim m_0^2\langle\bar qq\rangle$ with $m_0^2 \approx 0.8 \text{ GeV}^2$; subleading correction

active.explanation: Power corrections in OPE for a correlator at large $Q^2$: $\Pi(Q^2) = C_0(Q^2)\mathbb 1 + C_3(Q^2)\langle\bar qq\rangle/Q^3 + C_4(Q^2)\langle\alpha_s GG\rangle/Q^4 + C_5\langle\bar q\sigma Gq\rangle/Q^5 + \ldots$. Each higher operator gives $1/Q^n$ suppression. Wilson coefficients computed in perturbative QCD; condensates determined from data, lattice, or instanton models.

question: What's the physical idea behind SVZ (Shifman-Vainshtein-Zakharov) QCD sum rules?

options: Equate two representations of a correlator $\Pi(Q^2) = i\int d^4x e^{iqx}\langle T J(x)J(0)\rangle$: OPE at large $Q^2$ (perturbative + power corrections) versus dispersion relation over hadron spectrum (resonance + continuum). Borel transform suppresses higher resonances; extract resonance properties (masses, decay constants)

Use Schwinger-Dyson equations to solve QCD non-perturbatively for all hadron masses (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
Numerical integration of the QCD action on a Euclidean lattice (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship)

Apply Cutkosky rules to one-loop QCD diagrams; sum the imaginary parts (true only as a special case, not in general) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: SVZ (1979): bridge between quark-gluon and hadronic descriptions. (1) For a current $J$ creating hadron of interest (e.g.\ $J = \bar u\gamma_\mu d$ for $\rho$ meson), compute $\Pi(Q^2)$ via OPE: perturbative coefficient + condensate corrections. (2) Insert hadronic states: $\Pi(Q^2) = \sum_n |\langle 0|J|n\rangle|^2/(m_n^2 + Q^2) + $ continuum. (3) Apply Borel transform $\hat B$ in $Q^2 \to M_B^2$: factorially suppresses higher resonances, suppresses high-dim OPE corrections. Match to extract $f_\rho, m_\rho$. Original SVZ paper: $m_\rho \approx 770$ MeV from $\langle\alpha_s GG\rangle/Q^4$ correction.

question: In deep inelastic scattering (DIS), the OPE for $J^\mu(x) J^\nu(0)$ at light-like separation gives moments of structure functions in terms of...?

options: Matrix elements of twist-2 operators $\bar\psi\gamma^{\{\mu_1}D^{\mu_2}\cdots D^{\mu_n\}}\psi$ (symmetric, traceless) â€” parton distribution moments; twist = dimension âˆ’ spin; higher twist = $1/Q^2$ suppressed corrections
Just the quark masses $m_q$ (not supported by the evidence) (a common misconception) (this conflates cause and effect)
Pion decay constant $f_\pi$ alone (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
Wilson loops of the gauge field along light-like contours (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this describes a different phenomenon)
passive.explanation: Light-cone OPE for DIS: $J^\mu(x) J^\nu(0) \sim \sum_n C_n^{\mu\nu}(x) \mathcal O_n(0)$ where the relevant operators are bilocal quark-quark and gluon-gluon operators. Expanded in twist $\tau = d - s$ (dimension minus spin): twist-2 dominate at large $Q^2$. Mellin moments of structure functions: $\int_0^1 x^{n-1} F_2(x,Q^2) dx = C_n(Q^2/\mu^2, \alpha_s) \langle\mathcal O_n(\mu)\rangle$. Wilson coefficients computed perturbatively; matrix elements = parton-distribution moments (non-perturbative, fit to data). Higher twist (3, 4, â€¦): $1/Q^{2(\tau-2)}$ suppressed corrections. Foundation for DGLAP evolution and global PDF fits.

question: The $\tau \to \nu_\tau + \text{hadrons}$ branching ratio gives one of the cleanest extractions of $\alpha_s$. Why is this 'inclusive' OPE prediction so reliable?

options: Inclusive ratio $R_\tau$ is dispersion integral; OPE applies on circle $|s| = m_\tau^2$ in complex plane avoiding resonances; perturbative QCD up to $\alpha_s^4$; power corrections small ($m_\tau^2 \sim 3 \text{ GeV}^2$ marginal); yields $\alpha_s(m_\tau) \approx 0.33$, equivalent to $\alpha_s(m_Z) \approx 0.120$
$\tau$ decay involves only leptons, so perturbative QED suffices (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
Hadronic $\tau$ decay has no QCD corrections â€” pure electroweak (this reverses the actual relationship) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (not supported by the evidence)
$R_\tau$ is dominated by the $\rho$ meson, which is well-measured (this conflates cause and effect) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a common misconception)
passive.explanation:
$R_	au = \Gamma(	au	o
u+	ext{hadrons})/\Gamma(	au	o
u_	au e ar
u_e)$. Express as contour integral of correlator $\Pi(s)$ around $|s| = m_	au^2$ in complex $s$-plane (Cauchy theorem). On the circle, $|s|$ large $\Rightarrow$ OPE valid. Contour avoids the physical cut where resonances are messy. Perturbative series known to $\alpha_s^4$ (Baikov-Chetyrkin-KÃ¼hn). Power corrections at dim 4 ($\langle GGangle/m_	au^4$) and dim 6 ($\langle ar qqangle^2/m_	au^6$): few percent each. Result: $\alpha_s(m_	au) = 0.325 \pm 0.015$, runs to $\alpha_s(m_Z) = 0.1180 \pm 0.0008$ â€” competitive with lattice and $Z$-pole values.

question: Heavy-quark expansion (HQE) for inclusive $B$ decays uses an OPE in $1/m_b$. What's the leading non-trivial correction?

options: No $1/m_b$ correction (eliminated by equations of motion); leading correction at $1/m_b^2$ from kinetic operator $\mu_\pi^2 = -\langle B|\bar b(iD)^2 b|B\rangle/(2m_B)$ and chromomagnetic $\mu_G^2 = \langle B|\bar b\sigma\cdot G b|B\rangle/(2m_B)$ â€” few percent corrections to free-quark result
Leading $1/m_b$ correction from the quark mass alone (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon)
OPE in $1/m_b$ doesn't apply to $B$ decays â€” they're purely non-perturbative (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this conflates cause and effect)
Leading correction is $\alpha_s$ at order $1/m_b^0$, larger than all power corrections (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
passive.explanation: HQE for inclusive semileptonic $B\to X_c\ell\nu$ width: $\Gamma = \Gamma_0[1 + 0/m_b + c_2 \mu_\pi^2/m_b^2 + c_2' \mu_G^2/m_b^2 + c_3 \rho^3/m_b^3 + \ldots]\,$. Leading $\Gamma_0$ = free-quark decay. No $1/m_b$ term (Chay-Georgi-Grinstein theorem): use $iD^\mu \to (v\cdot iD)v^\mu + \ldots$ and EOM. $\mu_\pi^2 \sim 0.4 \text{ GeV}^2$ (kinetic energy of $b$ in $B$), $\mu_G^2 \sim 0.35 \text{ GeV}^2$ (chromomagnetic, from $B^* - B$ splitting). Extracts $|V_{cb}|$ to ~1% precision. Foundation: heavy quark effective theory (HQET).

question: Renormalons are factorial growth of perturbative coefficients signalling sensitivity to OPE condensates. Explain the IR renormalon.

options: Perturbative series for correlator has coefficients $a_n \sim n!(\beta_0/2)^n/Q^{2k}$ from bubble chains; Borel transform has poles on positive real axis at $u = k$ â†’ ambiguity $\sim e^{-2k/\beta_0\alpha_s} = (\Lambda_{\rm QCD}^2/Q^2)^k$; precisely matched (cancelled) by ambiguity in dimension-$2k$ condensate $\Rightarrow$ OPE + perturbation theory consistent

UV renormalons signal Landau poles and require new physics (a common misconception) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this describes a different phenomenon) (not supported by the evidence)

Renormalons are spurious artefacts of dimensional regularisation only (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this conflates cause and effect) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
Renormalons appear in QED but not in QCD (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this reverses the actual relationship) (a common misconception)
passive.explanation: Renormalons (`t Hooft, Beneke): in QCD, summing fermion-bubble chains gives $a_n \sim n! K^n$ with $K = -2/\beta_0$ for IR renormalon, $K = +2/\beta_0$ for UV renormalon (in $\beta_0 = (11N_c - 2N_f)/3 > 0$). IR renormalon at Borel parameter $u = k$ produces ambiguity $\delta = e^{-2k/\beta_0\alpha_s(Q)} = (\Lambda/Q)^{2k}$ exactly the size of dim-$2k$ condensate contribution to OPE. Resolution: Wilson coefficient $C_n$ and matrix element $\langle\mathcal O_n\rangle$ are individually ambiguous, but their product is unambiguous when condensate is defined consistently with perturbative scheme. Famous example: pole-mass renormalon at $u = 1/2$, $\delta m_b \sim \Lambda_{\rm QCD}$, motivates switching to MS-bar or kinetic mass.
